sections.content:
OK, now I see the pattern more clearly. After moving RIGHT:
1. Player moved from [29,28]-[31,31] to [28,32]-[31,35] (moved right by ~4 columns and up by 1 row)
2. The G frame at [24,28]-[27,31] converted back to c (player moved away)
3. The b frame at [28,32]-[31,39] was split into two parts: [28,28]-[31,31] and [28,36]-[31,39]
4. The gray bar at [29,32]-[30,38] was split into two parts: [29,29]-[30,31] and [29,36]-[30,38]
5. The player is now inside the b frame, between the two split parts
Looking at the ascii:
```
Row 28: ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb  (c frame, b frame, player N, W gap, b frame)
Row 29: cbbcbgggNNNWgggb  (c frame, b interior, gray bar, player N, W gap, gray bar, b border)
Row 30: cbbcbgggNNNWgggb  (same)
Row 31: ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb  (c frame, b frame, player N, W gap, b frame)
```
So the player is at cols 32-35 (NNNN), and there's a W gap at col 36. The b frame is at cols 28-31 (left part) and cols 37-39 (right part). The gray bar is at cols 29-31 (left part) and cols 37-38 (right part).
Wait, that doesn't match the segmentation. Let me re-read:
- b: [28,28]-[31,28] - this is the left b frame part
- b: [28,36]-[28,38] - this is the right b frame part (but only row 28?)
Actually, the boundary format is [top-left] to [bottom-left] (first and last corners). So:
- b: [28,28]-[31,28] means rows 28-31, cols 28-31 (4x4 object, 10 pixels? That's weird for a 4x4)
Wait, 10 pixels for a 4x4 object doesn't make sense. Unless it's a hollow frame (border only).
Looking at the ascii:
- Row 28: ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb - cols 28-31 are bbbb (4 cells)
- Row 29: cbbcbgggNNNWgggb - cols 28-31 are bggg (b at 28, g at 29-31)
- Row 30: cbbcbgggNNNWgggb - cols 28-31 are bggg (b at 28, g at 29-31)
- Row 31: ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb - cols 28-31 are bbbb (4 cells)
So the b frame is a 4x4 hollow square: border is b, interior is g (gray bar). That's 4+1+1+4 = 10 pixels for the b border, which matches.
Similarly, the right b frame part:
- Row 28: ...bbbb - cols 37-39 are bbb (3 cells)
- Row 29: ...gggb - cols 37-39 are ggb (g at 37-38, b at 39)
- Row 30: ...gggb - cols 37-39 are ggb (g at 37-38, b at 39)
- Row 31: ...bbbb - cols 37-39 are bbb (3 cells)
Wait, that's only 3+2+2+3 = 10 pixels, which matches the segmentation's 10 pixels for the right b frame.
But the boundary says [28,36]-[28,38], which would be row 28, cols 36-38. That doesn't match the ascii. Let me re-check.
Actually, looking at the ascii more carefully:
Row 28: ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb
- cccc: cols 24-27
- bbbb: cols 28-31
- NNNN: cols 32-35
- W: col 36
- bbbb: cols 37-40
Wait, but the string is 16 chars long (cols 24-39). Let me count:
ccccbbbbNNNWbbbb
0123456789012345
- cccc: positions 0-3 (cols 24-27)
- bbbb: positions 4-7 (cols 28-31)
- NNNN: positions 8-11 (cols 32-35)
- W: position 12 (col 36)
- bbbb: positions 13-16... but the string is only 16 chars, so positions 13-15 (cols 37-39)
So the right b frame is at cols 37-39, which is 3 columns wide. But the segmentation says b: [28,36]-[28,38], which would be cols 36-38. Hmm, there's a discrepancy.
Actually, I think the segmentation boundary [28,36]-[28,38] means the object spans from row 28, col 36 to row 28, col 38. But that's just the top edge. The full object would span rows 28-31, cols 36-39 (or similar).
Let me not worry about the exact coordinates and focus on the game mechanics.
The key observation is that when the player moves RIGHT, it cuts through the b frame, splitting it into two parts. The player ends up between the two parts.
Now, looking at the current state:
- c frame at [24,28]-[27,31] (top)
- c frame at [28,16]-[31,19] (left)
- c frame at [28,24]-[31,27] (middle)
- Player at [28,32]-[31,35] (inside b frame)
- b frame split into left and right parts
- gray bar split into left and right parts
The player can now move LEFT or RIGHT. Moving LEFT would move the player back toward the middle c frame. Moving RIGHT would move the player toward the right b frame part.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The player seems to be "eating" or "cutting" through the b frames. The c frames appear and disappear based on player movement.
Maybe the goal is to eat all the b frames and gray bars, leaving only the c frames? Or maybe the goal is to arrange the c frames in a specific pattern?
Let me try moving LEFT to see if the player can "eat" the middle c frame.
